stem:
A 6-week-old breastfed boy is admitted with a one-week history of progressive forceful vomiting that shoots out after feeds, with no green or yellow colour reported by his mother. He is feeding eagerly but losing weight. The general practitioner found a palpable upper abdominal lump during a feed yesterday. Bedside ultrasound performed in the emergency department this morning showed pyloric muscle thickness 4.5 mm and pyloric channel length 19 mm.

He is mildly dehydrated with reduced skin turgor and a sunken anterior fontanelle. Paediatric surgery has reviewed the patient and listed him for pyloromyotomy. The surgical team is asking whether they can proceed this afternoon or whether you would like to delay until tomorrow.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 162 /min, blood pressure 78/48 mmHg, respiratory rate 36 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: mildly dehydrated, sunken fontanelle, palpable olive-shaped mass in the upper abdomen, abdomen otherwise soft, no peritonism
data_table.Investigations: sodium 132 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 2.9 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), chloride 88 mmol/L (95-110), bicarbonate 34 mmol/L (22-32), urea 9.2 mmol/L (2.0-7.0), pH 7.52 base excess +9 mmol/L, ultrasound confirms pyloric stenosis

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management before pyloromyotomy?

options.text: Commence oral electrolyte rehydration solution while maintaining nil-by-mouth status overnight for next-day theatre
options.rationale: Oral intake in a child with gastric outlet obstruction is ineffective for resuscitation because the fluid will not pass the pylorus. Intravenous resuscitation is required. (Source: RCH CPG Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Proceed directly to theatre this afternoon and correct any residual electrolyte disturbances during anaesthesia
options.rationale: Operating on an alkalotic infant predisposes to post-anaesthetic apnoea. The dehydration is not an obstructed abdomen with bowel ischaemia; there is time to resuscitate. RCH guidance is clear that biochemical correction precedes operation. (Source: RCH CPG Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Insert a nasogastric tube on free drainage and operate within 4 hours regardless of current biochemistry result
options.rationale: NGT decompression is reasonable adjunct but does not address the metabolic alkalosis. Time-pressured operation in an alkalotic infant raises post-op apnoea risk. (Source: RCH CPG Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Correct the hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis with intravenous fluid resuscitation before theatre
options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis is a medical resuscitation that becomes a surgical case, not the reverse. The hypochloraemic, hypokalaemic, metabolic alkalosis must be corrected before general anaesthesia because alkalosis predisposes to post-operative apnoea through central respiratory depression. The accepted biochemical thresholds for theatre are bicarbonate below 30 and chloride above 100 mmol/L. (Source: RCH CPG Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Commence ranitidine 1 mg/kg intravenously for gastric acid suppression and proceed to theatre this evening
options.rationale: Acid suppression does not address the urgent biochemical derangement that drives the post-anaesthetic apnoea risk. It is also not part of the standard pre-operative pathway. (Source: RCH CPG Pyloric stenosis)

explanation.summary: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis classically produces a hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis from loss of gastric hydrochloric acid in the vomitus. The alkalosis is a contraindication to general anaesthesia because of post-operative apnoea risk, so the infant is resuscitated to biochemical safety before pyloromyotomy. Targets are bicarbonate below 30 mmol/L and chloride above 100 mmol/L. Resuscitation fluid is 0.9 percent saline with potassium replacement once urine output is established.
key_points: Pre-operative biochemistry targets: bicarbonate below 30 mmol/L and chloride above 100 mmol/L.
Initial fluid is 0.9 percent saline with potassium replacement once urine output established.
Alkalosis predisposes to central respiratory depression after anaesthesia.
Pyloric stenosis is not a surgical emergency.
explanation.pearls: Correct the hypochloraemic, hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis of pyloric stenosis with 0.9 percent saline and potassium replacement before pyloromyotomy; aim for bicarbonate below 30 mmol/L and chloride above 100 mmol/L.

stem:
A 4-year-old girl is brought to the general practice clinic with 8 months of stool withholding, infrequent painful bowel motions every 5 to 7 days, and recent daily soiling of small amounts of liquid stool into her underwear. Her mother says her tummy is always full and hard. She is otherwise well, eats a varied diet, and is on the 50th centile for height and weight.

Examination shows an alert child with a non-tender, distended abdomen and a palpable faecal mass in the left iliac fossa. Anal inspection is normal with no fissure and no sacral pit. There are no red flags for organic disease (no failure to thrive, no delayed first stool in the newborn period, no green vomiting, no neurological signs). Her current management is occasional lactulose with limited adherence.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 96 /min, blood pressure 100/62 mmHg, respiratory rate 22 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: soft non-tender abdomen with palpable left iliac fossa faecal mass, normal anal inspection, no sacral dimple or tuft of hair, normal lower limb tone and reflexes
data_table.Investigations: clinical diagnosis, no imaging or bloods required

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue lactulose at the current dose and emphasise dietary fibre and fluid intake at follow-up in 4 weeks time
options.rationale: Dietary advice and a low-dose osmotic alone are inadequate where there is established faecal impaction. The withheld stool reservoir must be cleared before maintenance becomes effective. (Source: RCH CPG Constipation)

options.text: Disimpaction with macrogol 3350 with electrolytes at an escalating regimen over 3 to 7 days then daily maintenance
options.rationale: Functional constipation with faecal impaction requires disimpaction before maintenance. RCH recommends macrogol 3350 with electrolytes (Movicol Junior or equivalent) at an escalating dose over 3 to 7 days, then a daily maintenance dose for at least 6 months. Maintenance therapy alone without disimpaction repeatedly fails. (Source: RCH CPG Constipation)

options.text: Administer a daily phosphate enema for 3 days in clinic to clear the impaction before commencing maintenance therapy
options.rationale: Phosphate enemas are associated with electrolyte derangement and trauma in children and are not first-line for disimpaction in the AU paediatric pathway. Oral macrogol disimpaction is preferred. (Source: RCH CPG Constipation)

options.text: Commence senna 2.5 mL nocte as a stimulant laxative for 4 weeks and review symptoms at the end of that course
options.rationale: Stimulant laxatives alone are not first-line for disimpaction. They are useful as adjuncts to osmotics in selected maintenance regimens but do not clear faecal mass effectively. (Source: RCH CPG Constipation)

options.text: Refer for urgent paediatric gastroenterology review and arrange an abdominal x-ray to confirm faecal impaction
options.rationale: Faecal impaction is a clinical diagnosis confirmed by abdominal examination and history. Abdominal x-ray is not routinely required and adds radiation without changing management. The presentation lacks red flags for organic disease that would justify subspecialist referral. (Source: RCH CPG Constipation)

explanation.summary: Functional constipation with palpable faecal mass requires oral disimpaction with macrogol 3350 with electrolytes at an escalating dose for 3 to 7 days, followed by daily maintenance for at least 6 months. Failure to disimpact before maintenance is the most common reason for treatment failure. Dietary advice and a low-dose osmotic alone are inadequate when impaction is already established. Red flags for organic disease prompt further investigation but are absent in this child.
key_points: Disimpaction: macrogol 3350 with electrolytes, escalating regimen over 3 to 7 days.
Maintenance: macrogol 3350 daily for at least 6 months, weaned gradually.
Red flags for organic disease: failure to thrive, delayed meconium, bilious vomiting, abnormal neurology, sacral dimple or hair tuft.
Abdominal x-ray is not required when the abdomen is examinable.
explanation.pearls: Use macrogol 3350 with electrolytes for both disimpaction (escalating dose over 3 to 7 days) and maintenance (daily dose for 6 months); enemas and dietary advice alone fail because the withheld stool reservoir is not cleared.

stem:
A 7-year-old boy presents to the paediatric outpatient clinic for review 1 week after discharge with a diagnosis of Henoch-Schonlein purpura. He had palpable purpura over the buttocks and extensor surfaces of the lower legs, bilateral ankle pain that has resolved, and 3 days of cramping tummy pain that came in waves that settled with simple analgesia. He never had macroscopic haematuria.

At admission his urinalysis showed trace blood and no proteinuria, his blood pressure was at the 50th centile, and his renal function was normal. Today he is well. His mother asks how often you need to check his urine and blood pressure from here.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 92 /min, blood pressure 100/64 mmHg (50th centile for age and height), respiratory rate 20 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.5 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: fading non-blanching purpura on buttocks and lower legs, no joint swelling, no abdominal tenderness, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: urinalysis trace blood no protein, urine albumin creatinine ratio less than 2 mg/mmol, creatinine 38 micromol/L (paediatric range), blood pressure on growth chart at 50th centile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate renal follow-up plan?

options.text: Renal ultrasound at 3 months and at 6 months as the primary screening tool for evolving nephritis or scarring
options.rationale: Renal imaging is reserved for established nephritis or in atypical clinical course. The primary screen is dipstick urinalysis with quantitative urine ACR or PCR if positive, plus blood pressure. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Repeat urinalysis only if macroscopic haematuria, peripheral oedema or hypertension develop in the next 6 months
options.rationale: HSP nephritis can be silent in its early stages with microscopic haematuria or low-grade proteinuria as the only signs. Waiting for symptomatic disease misses the treatment window. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Weekly urinalysis and blood pressure measurement for 2 months then monthly until 6 months from initial onset
options.rationale: RCH guidance recommends close renal surveillance after HSP because nephritis can develop late (up to 6 months after onset). Urinalysis and blood pressure weekly for the first 1 to 2 months, then monthly to 6 months, picks up evolving nephritis at the point where treatment intensification is meaningful. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Daily urinalysis for 6 weeks at home using parent-performed dipstick testing with weekly clinic blood pressure
options.rationale: Daily urinalysis is impractical and not supported by evidence. Weekly testing in the first months catches evolving disease without burdening the family. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Refer for percutaneous renal biopsy now to define histological prognosis before any further surveillance plan
options.rationale: Renal biopsy is reserved for nephrotic-range proteinuria, hypertension, or progressive renal dysfunction. A child with HSP and no renal findings does not need a biopsy. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

explanation.summary: Henoch-Schonlein purpura nephritis develops up to 6 months after initial presentation and can be silent in its early stages. RCH recommends weekly urinalysis and blood pressure for the first 1 to 2 months, then monthly to 6 months, with escalation if proteinuria, hypertension or worsening renal function emerges. Biopsy is reserved for significant proteinuria, hypertension, or rising creatinine.
key_points: Renal surveillance after HSP runs for 6 months from onset.
Weekly urinalysis and BP for first 1 to 2 months, then monthly to 6 months.
Quantify proteinuria with ACR or PCR if dipstick positive.
Biopsy indications: nephrotic-range proteinuria, hypertension, declining renal function.
explanation.pearls: Schedule weekly urinalysis and blood pressure for 1 to 2 months then monthly to 6 months in HSP; nephritis can be silent and biopsy is reserved for nephrotic-range proteinuria or progressive disease.

subtopic_detail: Incomplete Kawasaki recognition and IVIG timing
stem:
An 11-month-old boy is admitted with 9 days of fever to 39 to 40 degrees Celsius. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, perineal desquamation, and a single right cervical lymph node 1.8 cm in diameter. He has no rash, no oral mucosal changes, and no extremity changes. He has been investigated for sepsis with negative urine, blood, and respiratory virus cultures.

C-reactive protein is 142 mg/L, ESR 78 mm/hour, platelets 612 x 10^9/L, albumin 28 g/L, ALT 78 U/L, and urine has sterile pyuria. The paediatric cardiology echocardiogram performed today shows a left anterior descending coronary artery z-score of 2.6 with perivascular brightness.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 158 /min, blood pressure 94/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 36 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 38.9 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, perineal desquamation, single tender right cervical lymph node 1.8 cm, no rash, no oral changes, no extremity oedema
data_table.Investigations: CRP 142 mg/L (under 5), ESR 78 mm/hour (under 15), platelets 612 x 10^9/L (150-450), albumin 28 g/L (35-45), ALT 78 U/L (under 40), urine sterile pyuria, echocardiogram LAD z-score 2.6 with perivascular brightness

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue observation with daily inflammatory markers and repeat echocardiogram in 1 week
options.rationale: Observation is not appropriate when the criteria for incomplete Kawasaki are met and coronary changes are present. Delay risks progression to coronary aneurysm. (Source: AHA Kawasaki disease scientific statement)

options.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 10 to 12 hours with high-dose aspirin therapy
options.rationale: This is incomplete Kawasaki disease - fever 5 or more days with 2 or 3 classical features, supportive laboratory findings, and now coronary artery z-score 2.5 or above on echocardiogram. IVIG 2 g/kg with aspirin should be commenced once the diagnosis is established, even after day 10 if there is ongoing inflammation or coronary involvement. (Source: AHA Kawasaki disease scientific statement; RCH CPG Kawasaki)

options.text: Commence intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg daily for 3 days as primary therapy without IVIG
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are an adjunct to or rescue therapy after IVIG, not a substitute for first-line treatment in non-high-risk patients. They are also used as primary adjunct in high-risk patients (Kobayashi score positive) but in addition to, not instead of, IVIG. (Source: AHA Kawasaki disease scientific statement)

options.text: Start infliximab 5 mg/kg as primary therapy in view of the high CRP and coronary z-score
options.rationale: Infliximab is reserved for IVIG-resistant Kawasaki after a first or second dose of IVIG has failed. It is not first-line therapy. (Source: AHA Kawasaki disease scientific statement)

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics and re-image at 48 hours before deciding on therapy
options.rationale: Broad antibiotics have been trialled empirically with no response and cultures are negative. Withholding IVIG to chase an occult bacterial source is not justified when criteria for incomplete Kawasaki are met. (Source: AHA Kawasaki disease scientific statement)

explanation.summary: Incomplete Kawasaki disease is diagnosed when there is fever for 5 or more days with 2 or 3 classical features plus supportive laboratory findings (raised CRP and ESR, anaemia, thrombocytosis, raised ALT, hypoalbuminaemia, sterile pyuria) or a coronary artery z-score of 2.5 or above. First-line treatment is IVIG 2 g/kg with aspirin. Treatment should not be delayed beyond day 10 if there is ongoing inflammation or evolving coronary involvement. Corticosteroids and infliximab are adjuncts or rescue therapy rather than substitutes for IVIG.
key_points: Incomplete Kawasaki: fever 5 or more days + 2 to 3 classical features + supportive labs OR coronary z-score 2.5 or above.
IVIG 2 g/kg with aspirin is first-line, even after day 10 if active inflammation persists.
Steroids are adjunct in high-risk patients (Kobayashi score positive) but not a substitute for IVIG.
Infliximab is for IVIG-resistant disease.
explanation.pearls: Incomplete Kawasaki with coronary z-score 2.5 or above mandates IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin even past day 10; steroids and biologics are adjuncts or rescue, not substitutes.

stem:
A 3-year-old girl is referred from her general practitioner with a 4-day history of bruising and a petechial rash. She had a viral upper respiratory illness 2 weeks ago. She is otherwise well, eating and drinking normally, and active at preschool. Examination shows scattered petechiae over the trunk and limbs and a few small bruises on her shins; there are no wet purpura, no mucosal bleeding, and no organomegaly or lymphadenopathy.

Full blood count shows isolated thrombocytopenia at 18 x 10^9/L with normal haemoglobin and white cell count. Blood film confirms isolated thrombocytopenia with normal red cells and white cells; there are no blasts. She has no family history of bleeding disorders and is up to date with immunisations. The haematology registrar has reviewed and confirmed the working diagnosis of immune thrombocytopenia.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 102 /min, blood pressure 96/58 mmHg, respiratory rate 22 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: well child, scattered petechiae over trunk and limbs, small bruises on shins, no wet purpura, no mucosal bleeding, no organomegaly, no lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: haemoglobin 122 g/L (110-140), white cells 7.8 x10^9/L (4-12), platelets 18 x10^9/L (150-450), blood film isolated thrombocytopenia with normal red and white cells and no blasts

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Give a platelet transfusion targeting a platelet count above 50 x10^9/L to provide short-term haemostatic cover
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion in ITP is reserved for life-threatening bleeding because transfused platelets are also rapidly destroyed by the autoantibody. It does not address the underlying immune mechanism. (Source: ASH ITP guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 0.8 g/kg as a single dose to raise the platelet count rapidly within hours
options.rationale: IVIG raises platelets faster but does not improve clinical outcomes in cutaneous-only ITP. It is reserved for active mucosal or organ bleeding and for procedures requiring haemostasis. (Source: ASH ITP guidelines)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 4 mg/kg/day for 4 days followed by a stepwise taper over the next 1 to 2 weeks
options.rationale: Corticosteroids raise platelet count but have steroid side effects and do not change long-term outcomes in cutaneous-only disease. Reserve for active bleeding or imminent procedures. (Source: ASH ITP guidelines)

options.text: Observation with written advice on bleeding precautions and arrange weekly platelet count for the next 4 weeks
options.rationale: Newly diagnosed paediatric ITP with cutaneous bleeding only (no wet purpura, no mucosal or major bleeding) is managed with observation regardless of platelet count. Bleeding outcomes correlate with bleeding phenotype, not the platelet number. Most cases resolve within 6 months. (Source: ASH ITP guidelines; RCH CPG ITP)

options.text: Refer for urgent bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy to exclude an underlying haematological malignancy
options.rationale: Bone marrow biopsy is not required when the clinical picture and blood film are typical of ITP. Reserve for atypical features such as lymphadenopathy, organomegaly, abnormal red or white cell line, or failure to respond to treatment. (Source: ASH ITP guidelines)

explanation.summary: Newly diagnosed paediatric ITP with cutaneous bleeding alone is managed with observation, bleeding precautions, and serial platelet counts irrespective of the absolute platelet number. Active treatment (IVIG, steroids, anti-D) is reserved for mucosal or organ bleeding or for procedures. Bone marrow biopsy is not routine when the clinical and laboratory picture is typical.
key_points: Treatment decisions in ITP are guided by bleeding phenotype, not platelet count.
Cutaneous-only bleeding is managed with observation even if platelets are below 20 x10^9/L.
IVIG, steroids, and anti-D are reserved for mucosal or organ bleeding.
Most paediatric ITP resolves within 6 months.
explanation.pearls: Manage cutaneous-only newly diagnosed paediatric ITP with watch and wait plus bleeding precautions; treat only for wet purpura, mucosal bleeding, or procedure preparation per ASH paediatric ITP guidance.

subtopic_detail: New T1DM without DKA - initiation approach
stem:
A 9-year-old boy is referred by his general practitioner with 4 weeks of polyuria, polydipsia, and 3 kg weight loss. A clinic capillary blood glucose was 22 mmol/L. He is alert, ambulating, and tolerating oral fluids. The general practitioner sent him to the emergency department after capillary ketones returned 0.6 mmol/L.

In the emergency department his repeat capillary blood glucose is 19 mmol/L, capillary ketones are 0.5 mmol/L, and venous blood gas shows pH 7.36 and bicarbonate 23 mmol/L. He is haemodynamically stable, mucous membranes are moist, and abdominal examination is normal. The on-call paediatric endocrinology consultant has reviewed and confirmed this is new-onset Type 1 diabetes mellitus without diabetic ketoacidosis.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 96 /min, blood pressure 108/64 mmHg, respiratory rate 20 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: alert, moist mucous membranes, abdomen soft and non-tender, normal mental state, capillary refill under 2 seconds
data_table.Investigations: capillary blood glucose 19 mmol/L (under 7 fasting), capillary ketones 0.5 mmol/L (under 0.6), venous pH 7.36 (7.35-7.45), bicarbonate 23 mmol/L (22-32), sodium 136 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 4.1 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial insulin regimen?

options.text: Discharge for outpatient initiation of insulin by the community diabetes team in the next 24 to 48 hours
options.rationale: Newly diagnosed paediatric T1DM is admitted for inpatient initiation, diabetes education, parental skill development (injection technique, carbohydrate counting, hypoglycaemia management), and arrangement of community follow-up. (Source: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous fluid bolus 10 mL/kg of 0.9 percent saline followed by an insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/hour
options.rationale: An IV insulin infusion is the DKA pathway. This child does not meet DKA criteria (pH 7.36, bicarbonate 23, ketones 0.5). Treating him as DKA is overtreatment that risks hypoglycaemia and electrolyte disturbance. (Source: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines)

options.text: Twice-daily premixed insulin 30/70 with breakfast and dinner as a simpler starter regimen for the family
options.rationale: Twice-daily premixed insulin is less physiological and produces inferior glycaemic outcomes compared with basal-bolus in newly diagnosed paediatric T1DM. AU and ISPAD guidance favour basal-bolus or pump therapy from diagnosis. (Source: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines)

options.text: Oral metformin 500 mg twice daily as initial therapy and arrange outpatient endocrinology review in 1 week
options.rationale: Metformin is not first-line for Type 1 diabetes; insulin is mandatory because of absolute insulin deficiency. The diagnosis is clinically Type 1 based on age, weight loss, and ketosis. (Source: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines)

options.text: Subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin (long-acting once daily plus rapid-acting with meals) with diabetes education
options.rationale: Newly diagnosed Type 1 diabetes without DKA is treated with subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin (long-acting basal plus rapid-acting with meals) and diabetes education from the multidisciplinary team. IV insulin is reserved for DKA. The child is alert, eating, and not acidotic. (Source: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines)

explanation.summary: New-onset Type 1 diabetes without DKA is treated with subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin (long-acting once daily plus rapid-acting with meals), inpatient admission for multidisciplinary diabetes education, and structured outpatient follow-up. IV insulin and large fluid resuscitation belong to the DKA pathway. Treating this non-acidotic, clinically stable child as DKA overtreats and adds risk of hypoglycaemia. Basal-bolus is preferred over twice-daily premixed insulin from diagnosis under ISPAD guidance.
key_points: DKA requires venous pH below 7.30 or bicarbonate below 18 mmol/L plus ketosis.
Without DKA: subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin, oral hydration, education.
Basal-bolus is preferred over twice-daily premix from diagnosis.
Metformin has no role in established Type 1 diabetes.
explanation.pearls: New paediatric Type 1 diabetes without DKA gets subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin and inpatient education; IV insulin is the DKA pathway and is unnecessary here.
